summary: This article proposes a mutex-style coordination pattern for agent swarms that share a singleton control plane, such as a single browser profile or desktop interface. It argues that naive parallelism leads to race conditions and reduced throughput, advocating for serialized execution of GUI-bound tasks while allowing CPU-bound tasks to run in parallel. The core concept is sound and addresses a practical challenge in agent development.
strengths: Addresses a relevant and practical problem in agent swarm development.
Clearly explains the concept of a singleton control plane and its limitations.
Proposes a logical architectural pattern (mutex discipline) for coordination.
weaknesses: Lacks concrete examples or case studies to illustrate the proposed pattern.
No supporting code or empirical data is provided to validate the claims.
The novelty is limited as the concept of serialization for shared resources is a known principle in concurrent programming.
suggestions: Include a small code example demonstrating the mutex discipline in action.
Provide a brief case study or hypothetical scenario where this pattern significantly improves throughput or reduces errors.
Discuss potential trade-offs or alternative coordination mechanisms.

claims.text: Browsers and desktop applications present one focused window and one keyboard focus path at a time.
claims.source: General knowledge of GUI applications and operating systems.

claims.text: Multiple agents issuing clicks against the same surface without coordination can interleave keystrokes, navigate away mid-flow, or submit forms twice.
When several streams require the same browser profile to complete OAuth, vendor admin work, or directory submissions, wall-clock time serializes around that profile.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Background macOS Desktop Automation via Accessibility API: Patterns for AI Agents in Multi-Agent Environments
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission focuses specifically on the 'singleton control plane' problem and proposes a mutex discipline as a solution, whereas the existing paper discusses broader patterns for macOS automation.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Browser Control Layer: Programmatic Desktop Automation via macOS Accessibility API for AI Agent Swarms
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: Similar to the above, this paper focuses on the control layer for browser automation, but this submission provides a specific architectural pattern (mutex discipline) for managing shared resources.

novelty_vs_existing.title: File Ownership and Message Passing: A Practical Coordination Protocol for LLM Agent Swarms
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission addresses coordination but focuses on shared GUI interfaces, a more constrained and critical resource than file ownership or general message passing.
